request.question: The F Word was a romantic comedy film that starred the actor of what nationality, known for a role in "Life of Pi"?

title: Ashton Kutcher
Matthew Goode
Tom Cruise filmography
Jack of All Trades (2012 film)
Sandra Bullock filmography
Ed's Next Move
Liza Soberano
The F Word (2013 film)
Rafe Spall
Julia Roberts filmography
sentences: Christopher Ashton Kutcher ( ; born February 7, 1978) is an American actor and investor.
Kutcher began his career as a model and began his acting career portraying Michael Kelso in the Fox sitcom "That '70s Show," which aired for eight seasons.
He made his film debut in the romantic comedy "Coming Soon" and became known by audiences in the comedy film "Dude, Where's My Car?
", which was a box office hit.

In 2003, Kutcher appeared in romantic comedies "Just Married" and "My Boss's Daughter" and, the same year, he created, produced, and hosted "Punk'd" which aired on MTV for five seasons until its revival in 2012.
In 2004, Kutcher starred in the lead role of the psychological film "The Butterfly Effect" and gained public recognition.
Matthew William Goode (born 3 April 1978) is an English actor.
He made his screen debut in 2002 with ABC's TV movie feature "Confessions of an Ugly Stepsister".
His breakthrough role was in romantic comedy "Chasing Liberty" (2004), for which he received a nomination at Teen Choice Awards.
He then appeared in supporting roles in Woody Allen's "Match Point" (2005), the German-British romantic comedy "Imagine Me and You" (2006), the period drama "Copying Beethoven" (2006).

He gained praise for his performance as an aspiring artist in Julian Jarrold's drama "Brideshead Revisited" (2008) and as Ozymandias in the American neo-noir-superhero film "Watchmen" (2009), based on DC Comics' limited series of the same name.
He then starred in romantic comedy "Leap Year" (2010) and Australian drama "Burning Man" (2011), the latter earning him a nomination for Best Actor at the Film Critics Circle of Australia Awards.
Tom Cruise is an American actor and producer who made his film debut with a minor role in the 1981 romantic drama "Endless Love".

Two years later he made his breakthrough by starring in the romantic comedy "Risky Business" (1983), which garnered Cruise his first nomination for the Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy.

In 1986, Cruise played a fighter pilot in the Tony Scott-directed action drama "Top Gun" (the highest-grossing film that year), and also starred opposite Paul Newman in the Martin Scorsese-directed drama "The Color of Money".

Two years later he played opposite Dustin Hoffman in the Academy Award for Best Picture-winning drama "Rain Man" (1988), and also appeared in the Golden Raspberry Award for Worst Picture-winning romantic drama "Cocktail" (1988).
In doing so Cruise became the first and only person as of 2014 to star in a Best Picture Oscar winner and a Worst Picture Razzie winner in the same year.
His next role was as anti-war activist Ron Kovic in the drama adaptation of Kovic's memoir of the same name, "Born on the Fourth of July" (1989).
For his performance Cruise received the Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Drama and his first nomination for the Academy Award for Best Actor.
Jack of All Trades is a 2012 Chinese romantic comedy film directed by Pi Jianxin and written by Ma Zhiquan and Lin Manru, starring Wang Baoqiang, Eric Tsang, and Sherry Cao.
The film premiered in China on 8 March 2012.
Sandra Bullock is an American actress who made her film debut with a minor role in the 1987 thriller "Hangmen".

She made her television debut in the television film "Bionic Showdown: The Six Million Dollar Man and the Bionic Woman" (1989) and played the lead role in the short-lived sitcom "Working Girl" (1990) before making her breakthrough starring in the action film "Speed" (1994).
She starred with Sylvester Stallone in "Demolition Man" (1994).
Bullock founded her own production company, Fortis Films, and starred in the romantic comedy "While You Were Sleeping" in 1995.
Her performance in the film earned her first nomination for the Golden Globe Award for Best Actress – Motion Picture Comedy or Musical.
The following year, Bullock starred with Matthew McConaughey in the film adaptation of John Grisham's novel "A Time to Kill" (1996).
In 1997, she reprised her "Speed" role in the sequel, "".

The following year, Bullock starred in the romantic comedy "Practical Magic", voiced Miriam in the animated biblical film "The Prince of Egypt" and also executive produced her first film, the romantic drama "Hope Floats".
Ed's Next Move is a 1996 American romantic comedy film written and directed by John C. Walsh.
It stars Matt Ross and Callie Thorne.

A micro-budget romantic comedy about a transplanted Midwesterner adapting to life in New York's East Village, the film appeared at the 1996 Sundance Film Festival where it received critical praise and was picked up by Orion Classics for theatrical release.

The LA Times' Kenneth Turan called the movie "one of the most appealing, audience friendly films at Sundance," while Roger Ebert referred to the film as "a truth telling comedy with quiet wit and bright dialogue. "
Sight & Sound called it "a perfectly formed romantic comedy."
Hope Elizabeth "Liza" Soberano ( ; born January 4, 1998) is a Filipino-American actress and model.
She started in a range of television series and films, including "Wansapanataym" (2011), "Kung Ako'y Iiwan Mo" (2012), "She's the One" (2013), "Must Be... Love" (2013).
She rose to prominence after playing the protagonist Maria Agnes Calay in the romantic comedy television series "Forevermore" (2014–15) with Enrique Gil.

Since then, she has taken on starring roles with Gil in romantic comedy films "Just The Way You Are", based on the Wattpad book "The Bet", "Everyday I Love You" (both in 2015) and the television series "Dolce Amore" (2016).
In 2017, she starred in the romantic comedy drama film "My Ex and Whys", and is confirmed to star in "Darna" as the eponymous comic book superheroine.
The F Word (released in some countries as What If?)
is a 2013 Canadian romantic comedy film directed by Michael Dowse and written by Elan Mastai, based on TJ Dawe and Michael Rinaldi's play "Toothpaste and Cigars".
The film stars Daniel Radcliffe, Zoe Kazan, Megan Park, Adam Driver, Mackenzie Davis and Rafe Spall and follows a pair of best friends who begin to have feelings for each other.
Rafe Joseph Spall (born 10 March 1983) is an English actor.
He is best known for his roles in "The Shadow Line", "Pete versus Life", "One Day", "Anonymous", "Prometheus", "Life of Pi" and "The Big Short".
Julia Roberts is an American actress and producer who made her debut in the 1987 direct-to-video feature "Firehouse".
Roberts made her breakthrough the following year by starring in the coming-of-age film "Mystic Pizza" (1988).
For her supporting role in the comedy-drama "Steel Magnolias" (1989), she received the Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actress.
Roberts' next role was opposite Richard Gere in the romantic comedy "Pretty Woman" (1990).
The film is estimated to have sold over 42 million tickets in North America—the most for a romantic comedy in the United States as of 2014.
For her performance, Roberts won the Golden Globe Award for Best Actress (Musical or Comedy).
In 1991, she appeared in the psychological thriller "Sleeping with the Enemy", and played Tinker Bell in the Steven Spielberg-directed fantasy adventure "Hook".
Two years later, Roberts starred in the legal thriller "The Pelican Brief", an adaptation of the John Grisham novel of the same name.
During the late 1990s, she played the lead in the romantic comedies "My Best Friend's Wedding" (1997), "Notting Hill" (1999), and "Runaway Bride" (1999).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: The F Word was a romantic comedy film that starred the actor of what nationality, known for a role in "Life of Pi"?
hop1_docs: What the Jatt!! | What the Jatt!!  is a 2015 Punjabi romantic comedy film starring Harish Verma, Isha Rikhi, Vipul Roy and Poonam Rajput in lead roles.  Directed by Saket S Behl, the film was released worldwide on 13 March 2015.

Windrider | Windrider (alternatively worded as Wind Rider and also known as Making Waves) is a 1986 Australian romantic comedy film directed by Vincent Monton and starring Tom Burlinson.  Filmed in Perth, Western Australia, Australia, it features Nicole Kidman in her first 'adult' role and includes a few nude scenes.

What a Man (1930 film) | What a Man is a 1930 American romantic comedy film directed by George Crone and starring Reginald Denny, Miriam Seegar and Harvey Clark.  It was an adaptation of the play "They All Want Something" by Courtenay Savage, which was itself based on a novel by E.J. Rath.  A separate Spanish language version "Thus Is Life" was made at the same time.  The film was remade in 1938 as "Merrily We Live".  It is also known by the alternative title The Gentleman Chauffeur.

Byun Yo-han | Byun Yo-han (born April 29, 1986) is a South Korean actor.  He made his acting debut in 2011, and appeared in over 30 short films as a student of Korea National University of Arts.  The then-unknown actor rose to fame in 2014 with a supporting role in the popular workplace cable series "Misaeng".  In 2015, he starred in leading roles in indie hit "Socialphobia" and the romantic comedy "Ex-Girlfriend Club".

Robert Prescott (actor) | Robert Prescott is an American actor who has starred in various roles in film and on television.  He is best known for his role as Kent in the 1985 hit comedy film "Real Genius".  He also starred in the 1984 comedy film "Bachelor Party" as Cole Whittier, and appeared in the 1987 Mel Brooks hit comedy "Spaceballs" in a cameo as the Sand Cruiser Driver.

The F Word (2013 film) | The F Word (released in some countries as What If?)  is a 2013 Canadian romantic comedy film directed by Michael Dowse and written by Elan Mastai, based on TJ Dawe and Michael Rinaldi's play "Toothpaste and Cigars".  The film stars Daniel Radcliffe, Zoe Kazan, Megan Park, Adam Driver, Mackenzie Davis and Rafe Spall and follows a pair of best friends who begin to have feelings for each other.

Hugh Jackman | Hugh Michael Jackman (born 12 October 1968) is an Australian actor, singer, and producer.  Jackman has won international recognition for his roles in a variety of film genres.  He is known for his long-running role as Wolverine in the "X-Men" film series, as well as for his lead roles in films such as the romantic-comedy fantasy "Kate & Leopold" (2001), the action-horror film "Van Helsing" (2004), the magic-themed drama "The Prestige" (2006), the epic fantasy drama "The Fountain" (2006), the epic historical romantic drama "Australia" (2008), the film version of "Les Misérables" (2012), and the thriller "Prisoners" (2013).  His work in "Les Misérables" earned him his first Academy Award nomination for Best Actor and his first Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy in 2013.

  - No passages mention "Life of Pi" or connect any F Word cast member to that film (no matching fact tokens for Life of Pi in passages).
- Ambiguities:
  - The F Word cast includes multiple actors: "Daniel Radcliffe", "Zoe Kazan", "Megan Park", "Adam Driver", "Mackenzie Davis", and "Rafe Spall" (all from passage [6]). The passages do not indicate which (if any) of these is the actor "known for a role in 'Life of Pi'".
  - Expected pages for known Life of Pi actors (e.g., Suraj Sharma, Irrfan Khan, etc.) are not present in the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Candidate bridge entity (as it appears in the passages): "Rafe Spall" (passage [6]) — he is a plausible link but the passages do not state he was in Life of Pi.
  - Precise missing fact needed: Which specific F Word cast member appeared in the film Life of Pi, and that actor's nationality (e.g., "nationality of [Actor Name]").
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Rafe Spall" page — check lede/infobox and filmography for a credit in "Life of Pi" and for his nationality (search keywords: "Rafe Spall Life of Pi nationality").
  2) Open "Life of Pi (film)" page — check the Cast section to see which actor(s) overlap with The F Word cast (search keywords: "Life of Pi cast Rafe Spall cast list").
  3) If no match found for Rafe Spall, open pages for other F Word cast members ("Daniel Radcliffe", "Adam Driver", "Zoe Kazan", "Mackenzie Davis", "Megan Park") and check their ledes/filmographies for a "Life of Pi" credit and nationality.
  4) Re-open "The F Word (2013 film)" cast section (lede/infobox) to confirm the exact credited names if needed.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the above pages/sections.
response.hop2_query: Which actor appeared in both 'The F Word' and 'Life of Pi'?
hop2_docs: Gou pi gao | Gou Pi Gao (), is a medicinal herbal plaster that is often used in traditional Chinese medicine.  Gou Pi Gao, translated word for word, literally means dog skin plaster, which is indicative of its history.

Life of Pi (film) | Life of Pi is a 2012 American survival drama film based on Yann Martel's 2001 novel of the same name.  Directed by Ang Lee, the film's adapted screenplay was written by David Magee, and it stars Suraj Sharma, Irrfan Khan, Rafe Spall, Tabu, Adil Hussain, and Gérard Depardieu.  The storyline revolves around an Indian man named "Pi" Patel, telling a novelist about his life story, and how at 16 he survives a shipwreck in which his family dies, and is adrift in the Pacific Ocean on a lifeboat with a Bengal tiger.  The film had its worldwide premiere as the opening film of the 51st New York Film Festival at both the Walter Reade Theater and Alice Tully Hall in New York City on September 28, 2012.

Suraj Sharma | Suraj Sharma (born 21 March 1993) is an Indian actor, who made his debut with 2012 film "Life of Pi".  Directed by Ang Lee, "Life of Pi" was adapted from the novel of the same nameand earned him critical acclaim as well as the BAFTA Rising Star Award nomination.  In 2014, he portrayed Aayan Ibrahim in season 4 of the Showtime series "Homeland".

Ayush Tandon | Ayush Tandon (born 31 July 1998) is a film actor working in Bollywood and Hollywood films.  He played Pi, age 11 in the 2012 Hollywood film "Life of Pi".  He made his acting debut in the 2011 Bollywood film "7 Khoon Maaf" At the age of 10 he, along with Vasundhara Raturi entered as contestants in the first season of "Chota Packet Bada Dhamaka" and were declared as the winning jodi (team) during the grand finale which took place on place on 3 January 2009.

Rafe Spall | Rafe Joseph Spall (born 10 March 1983) is an English actor.  He is best known for his roles in "The Shadow Line", "Pete versus Life", "One Day", "Anonymous", "Prometheus", "Life of Pi" and "The Big Short".

Daniel Magder | Daniel Robbie Magder (born December 12, 1991) is a Canadian actor.  He has appeared in such projects such as "The Famous Jett Jackson", and "X-Men".  One of his most recent roles is on "Life with Derek", where he portrays Edwin Venturi.  He graduated Thornlea Secondary School in Thornhill, Ontario.  After attending the University of British Columbia for his first year, he transferred to the Vancouver Film School's writing for film and television course, which he completed in December 2011.  He is a brother of the Alpha Epsilon Pi Fraternity, Beta Chi chapter.  In 2016 he became the host of a popular trivia game called BINGPOT!

Adil Hussain | Adil Hussain ] , (born 5 October 1963) is an Indian stage, television and film actor from the state of Assam, who works in mainstream Bollywood as well as art house cinema.  He has worked in International Film like "The Reluctant Fundamentalist" and "Life of Pi" (both 2012) and he received the Special Jury Award at the 2017 National Film Awards.  He has acted in English, Hindi, Assamese, Bengali, Tamil, Marathi, Malayalam, Norwegian and French films.
response.summary_2: The F Word (2013) stars Daniel Radcliffe, Zoe Kazan, Megan Park, Adam Driver, Mackenzie Davis, and Rafe Spall. Rafe Joseph Spall is an English actor and is best known for roles including Life of Pi.
